item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The opposite of an amnestic is mnestic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To start, thank you for the detailed criticism.

To address a couple of points:

This wasn't a mainlister, because it felt too…large for that. Secondly, it was the closest I could come in the timeframe to produce a 3000. Hopefully it does better as a mainlister after the contest is over.

I don't think the narrative was pointlessly cruel, but that may have depended on seeing the whole thing from Kensington's perspective in the tale series. If I could go back and do it again, I'd probably either have included additional material to make it more obvious, or changed the subject matter to not depend on that. Looking back, that's a weakness, and that's my fault as a writer.

I do agree that "Skip what makes skips" is overused, but it made sense in the context. I felt that the narrative supported the trope, but it didn't quite work, and I accept that.

For what it's worth, this is supposed to be a relatively self contained explanation for the relative increase in skip production from a wiki perspective. A bit of meta humor, which may have been too clumsy.

Either way, I suggest you read the tale series, if only because…well, I'd like you to read it, and thank you for taking the time to read through my entry all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wanted to like this, because I really enjoy clever use of old/potentially-silly Foundation tripes. But I ended up really disliking it.

The narrative was too predictable, and I felt it treated its characters in ways that only made sense if you take into account that Michael Magnus is an author avatar (and a very important one … to the author of the page). (Note: This would still be the case even if the name were changed).

The narrative also felt pointlessly cruel to the ex-O5 in a way I found tiresome and overdone (and therefore not chilling or compelling or anything of the sort). Kensington is the only character given any characterization, and he feels really generic — no real personality. So why use the character — and why have the note, written by any character?

And while I like the anomalous physics elements, the creation-of-other-anomalies trope has been done over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. and over. again. That means I need something about it to be independently interesting. (And yes, that still applies given that I don't think this is meant to be a Creation of All Anomalies thing, but a Creation of Some New Anomalies thing.) This version of it left me completely cold. It would also be a deeply disappointing explanation for anomalies outside this SCP's story… and, honestly, within this SCP's story, too. And since you make several logic leaps to force it to function in the story, it feels like wasted effort.

I wonder if I might like this more if I read the tale series — but an SCP should stand on its own. It can absolutely be intrinsically tied to a greater narrative, and I also really like the idea of tying this to a tale series. But presently it makes me not want to read the tale series.

Also, as an SCP-3000 entry, this isn't really all that scary. Why wasn't this just a mainlist SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough, I've edited to a softer form.

Especially considering you didn't actually name anything you feel fits those two categories.

By reality physics elements, I mean Humes, SRAs and generic "reality bending" which in general are elements that I think were a mistake to introduce to the setting in the first place. By things not quite coming together for me, I don't find the actions of Melanie or the 05s especially plausible. I also don't find this especially scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the pig thing: That's a twistedly brilliant idea. I could see even a surgeon-grade-near-psychopath getting the point that "You are now responsible for your charges not dying. End of story."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While you're certainly entitled to your opinion, I think it's a bit far to say I'm using "the usual lazy narrative devices" and that this is a "hot mess."

Especially considering you didn't actually name anything you feel fits those two categories. Broad-stokes criticism like that isn't very useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I think it's a bold move to try to tie LOLFoundation into the mainlist again, and the canon certianly is a source of horrific dark humor. Unfortunately this doesn't really work for me, the reality physics elements don't quite overcome my usual resistance to them, the various elements never come together for me.

Incidentally, I've seen Melanie's punishment in real life. Navy Fleet Medical Corpsmen (i.e. the medics attached to USMC units) must save a pig from a gunshot wound as part of a training exercise, and one our Docs failed this particular task. As a consequence he was required to write a letter to the pig's parents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why? Dramatic irony, actually.

He fooled the veritants, he'd shown sufficient trust, and he was a director-level staff before the opening. He was also directly involved in an extra-dimensional event. That was enough for me to hold the strings together of a story, at least to me.

Srsverse magnus' bones absorb the shattering of reality, in the shape of that afterimage, and with it, all that…"wrong"ness has to go somewhere. Hence new anomalies. This happens every time lolverse magnus dies, yes.

I'm glad you enjoyed! Apologies for whatever difficulties you had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking at the final note, are we to understand that Kensington made O5 after this? 'Cause like… why? He seems every bit as culpable as Melanie.

Is srsverse Magnus activating every time a lolverse Magnus dies? I bet he is.

Anyway, I like it, but I feel it's a bit opaque without the associated tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No need to apologise - I didn't interpret your response as hostile, and my own was more pointed than it needed to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough.

I can see why those things would break the narrative, and I'll admit some of the choices I made kind of bend a bit in favor of story.

For the record, I wrote most of that response when I was in the throes of fever from food poisoning, so sorry if I came off hostile at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, that was just commentary on a new amnestic-like thingy. It's a neat idea, you picked a good word to call it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that my argument centres on the question of "does SCP-3000 cause other anomalies?". I don't agree that the article shows this.

The foundation has direct evidence of what's causing the hume level spikes

I assume you're referring to "SCP-3000 occasionally generates a Hume field" - the "direct evidence" is presumably some form of Kant counter or other established magitech. I'm okay with that from a logical perspective, leaving aside discussion of the use of Humes etc.

the hume level spikes, which correspond exactly with the discovery of an anomaly … anomalies appear *After* the image of magnus dissapears in to the bones.

Yes, but correlation is not causation. It seems to me that you (and implicitly the O5s) are saying: "Hume level spikes, then anomalies are discovered, therefore SCP-3000 caused the anomalies."

That's like saying: "Birds fly south, then winter comes, therefore bird movement reduces temperature".

What evidence does the Foundation have that the Hume level spikes *cause* the anomalies to exist? Indeed, what evidence does the Foundation even have that the anomalies come into existence at the same time as the Hume spikes? I note that your use of "discovery/discovered" in the skip is counter-productive, as it suggests that the anomalies may have existed for some time before the Foundation discovers them (and therefore before the Hume spikes).

This isn't about vengeance. This isn't about justice. This is about finding the best use of Melanie DuMourne for the Foundation without exposing The Foundation to any additional risk

If the Ethics Committee isn't about justice, it doesn't deserve the name. If they genuinely consider that indefinite imprisonment and forced labour is not for the purposes of retribution (in a legal theory sense), then they come across as petty and hypocritical, in much the same way that Dr Kensington does. That undermines my suspension of disbelief, and also my sense that the Foundation is in the right.

I'm not just trying to nitpick. I think that if you ironed out some of this stuff, it would improve the story by giving the O5 a clearer justification for their actions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've missed two key points:

1) The foundation has direct evidence of what's causing the hume level spikes, which correspond exactly with the discovery of an anomaly. Perhaps it's not as clear cut as an actual scientific article may require, however, I took some artistic liberty for the sake of story. With that point, the rest of your argument kinda falls apart.

2) Melanie isn't being punished for the death of Magnus, which isn't her fault, by the way. She's being punished for going against the O5 council and is personally responsible for the fact that there's all these new anomalies in the world which are killing people.

3) They're not really looking to have her admit her wrongdoing. As it said in the very first line of the ethics commitee finding: "This isn't about vengeance. This isn't about justice. This is about finding the best use of Melanie DuMourne for the Foundation without exposing The Foundation to any additional risk". The stinger being that there /was/ an element of vindiction in there, by Dr. Kensington.

You missed the key point of the correlation, which is what the article hinges on. The argument that "the bones flash when anomalies appear" is kind of weak, considering that anomalies appear *After* the image of magnus dissapears in to the bones.

To be honest, I think a lot of your other arguments fall apart with that.

If you didn't like it, though, that's totally your prerogative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect I might be missing the point of this. My understanding is that Melanie used Dr Magnus' corpse to restructure reality, with the goal of helping the Foundation. This has different results in two universes:

1) In the LolFoundation universe, Dr Magnus is killed and physically resurrected, and thereby gives the Senior Staff their plot armour in that canon. Melanie is a hero, despite the constant killing of Dr Magnus.

2) In the mainlist universe, Dr Magnus is a screaming ghost, and his corpse has a Hume-glow at the same time as anomalies are discovered. I'm not sure why this SCP-3000 is Keter - there is seemingly no evidence that SCP-3000 creates anomalies (correlation is not causation), and indeed there is at least as much evidence that either the discovery of anomalies makes the corpse glow, or that SCP-3000 assists in the discovery of pre-existing anomalies.

Melanie is a villain, and is sentenced to life without parole (some labour) by the O5 council. But as there is no evidence that she has caused the creation of anomalies, the only reason for this punishment is the death (and possibly the unconfirmed post-death suffering) of Dr Magnus - which goes unremarked in the LolFoundation universe. Considering everything else the Foundation (and particularly the O5s) have mandated (whatever your headcanon), this seems like a disproportionate response.

More to the point, there's no sign that Melanie will acknowledge her wrongdoing. As far as she is concerned, she did the right thing, because (a) in this universe, it is an early warning system for the identification of anomalies, and is therefore worth the cost of Dr Magnus' life (and he was already dead, of course, so not even that cost); and (b) there is evidence that it worked even better in that other universe, so it was definitely worth the attempt here. The lack of acknowledgement of culpability makes the council's punishment particularly vindictive (it's not designed towards rehabilitation at all) and removes the potential for an emotional arc to Melanie's character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honest answer: putting dates on made the graph look extraordinarily squashed together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haven't read the tale series this article serves as a conclusion to, so there was some context missing when I read this. However, the build up is sufficiently paid off when the punishment is dealt, and the reader learns of the former O5-2's fate. And what a chilling fate it is.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why would the graph have the time axis redacted? it really serves no purpose, there's no reason why it would be redacted, and this is supposed to be an overseer document anyhow, so nothing should be redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lolFoundation has found its way back to the mainlist, but in a more amiable way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the writing in this immensely, but it's difficult for me to imagine it as SCP-3000, sinply hecause it dosen't work out of the fantastic canon you've built up within lolfoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you just got me to pore through all five preceding tales, looking for the difference between the two iterations. I think I'm glad that I didn't find any single thing that branched. I can't say this article should be 3000, but as someone who had passed on the lolfoundation canon entirely, you've convinced me to have another look at the old adventures of the Senior Staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fixed the proofing errors you highlighted, but there were a couple I was kind of wondering about what you meant.

Why did you highlight veritants? I'm using them as the opposite for "amnestic."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Universally, this image has been observed to be drawn towards SCP-3000, however, no sources of motile force have been observed in SCP-3000's containment chamber.

If it's just an image, why would there need to be a force to cause it to move?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I kind of started reflexively proofreading, then just kept on going, so you get all the corrections and commentary together.
– hide block

To date, sixteen anomalous objects have been discovered

Where?

veritants

o rly

the assistance of SCP-343's

Don't need the apostrophe-s. Also don't really need to link to Directive Legends more than once, unless that's done for some kind of effect.

…Wait, wait, is this document contained within dash-beta, which also references itself? O.o

in to concurrent

of these "regeneration"

Fully-body injuries

Loss of position, loss of privileges, loss of life.

That period should be a colon.

Dang, I like this Ethics Committee punishment, though. Might be the best part of the article. Especially because I thought the 20 dB requirement was something having to do with kindness for Magnus, and I was totally wrong. :D

And somehow, the last line marking Kens as the new O5-2 is a great punchline. :D Not exactly scary, though I do like this dual hell Magnus and duMourne are trapped in, for the sins of another reality. Probably upvote after contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was on the fence about how to feel; not having read the tale series, I definitely missed some context, but not enough context to not know what was going on overall. It didn't really grab me, though I was curious throughout. I think the final collapsible seals it for me, and truly sells what you're going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The buildup here is good, and the last note hit me hard. I liked seeing characters I am only used to seeing in goofy settings used here dead seriously. Well done.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hey all.

Credit to all the people who read this, I can't remember all the names at this point.

You don't strictly need to have read my tale series to know wtf is going on, it's all in front of you, but it'll give you more context. (starts at The Most Dangerous Game)

Thank you all, for helping me bring this whole shebang with the Directive Legends arc to a close.

EDIT: All images are either from other pages on the wiki, or created by myself (The graph), and as such should be CC compliant.

You guys can steal my graph at will <3
